Maloo Results: https://testing.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/related?jobs=lustre-reviews&builds=104182#redirect

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

(5 comments)

Could you please explain the reasoning behind this more.  Forcing a new SEQ on all of the MDT-OST connections is a very heavyweight operation, sometimes taking 30 minutes or more.  I think the urgency of getting the MDT precreate changes landed is behind us, so it would be better to fix this properly rather than continuing to make test script changes that are working around behavior issues in the code.

For overstriped files it seems the problem is that it not possible to start a new OST sequence in the middle of file creation, which is understandable I think.  What is the outcome if the sequence actually runs out during overstripe file creation?  Hopefully it is only that the file will create e.g. only 8 objects per OST that instead of 16 objects per OST, and not crashing or similar?  I think in _most_ cases this would be fairly rare (overstriping itself is mostly used only for benchmarking), and the default SEQ size is 32M objects, so it won't run out.

It would be best if the MDS itself checked that each OST had at least, say, 5x (to handle parallel creates) the overstripe count of OIDs left on each OST before starting to creating an overstriped file, and otherwise wrap the OST to the next SEQ (which it should do directly instead of creating/deleting junk objects).  This would actually solve the problem for *real* users of overstriped files, rather than papering over it in the test scripts.

Failing that, force_new_seq_all() should be optimized to avoid "burning through" all of the OIDs when this isn't needed.  Doing it only for OSTs that are low on OIDs, and *not* doing it for OSTs that have thousands of OIDs they could still use.

Per my other comments, I don't think this is actually the right fix for this issue, but there *are* improvements that could be done to speed up/eliminate the need force_new_seq{_all}().

Patch Set 1:

> Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1
> 
> (5 comments)
> 
> Could you please explain the reasoning behind this more.  Forcing a new SEQ on all of the MDT-OST connections is a very heavyweight operation, sometimes taking 30 minutes or more.  I think the urgency of getting the MDT precreate changes landed is behind us, so it would be better to fix this properly rather than continuing to make test script changes that are working around behavior issues in the code.
> 
> For overstriped files it seems the problem is that it not possible to start a new OST sequence in the middle of file creation, which is understandable I think.  What is the outcome if the sequence actually runs out during overstripe file creation?  Hopefully it is only that the file will create e.g. only 8 objects per OST that instead of 16 objects per OST, and not crashing or similar?  I think in _most_ cases this would be fairly rare (overstriping itself is mostly used only for benchmarking), and the default SEQ size is 32M objects, so it won't run out.
> 
> It would be best if the MDS itself checked that each OST had at least, say, 5x (to handle parallel creates) the overstripe count of OIDs left on each OST before starting to creating an overstriped file, and otherwise wrap the OST to the next SEQ (which it should do directly instead of creating/deleting junk objects).  This would actually solve the problem for *real* users of overstriped files, rather than papering over it in the test scripts.
> 
> Failing that, force_new_seq_all() should be optimized to avoid "burning through" all of the OIDs when this isn't needed.  Doing it only for OSTs that are low on OIDs, and *not* doing it for OSTs that have thousands of OIDs they could still use.
> 
> Per my other comments, I don't think this is actually the right fix for this issue, but there *are* improvements that could be done to speed up/eliminate the need force_new_seq{_all}().

I read the server log wrong, I think I saw sequence rollover right after 130g failed, which means the current precreate pool was used up by the overstriping create.

Now checking the log again the most recent sequence rollover happened during 124a, which is recent from 130g, this means we should have enough objects in the current sequence.

I also thought about getting rid of force_new_seq_all for the overstriping tests, one way would be in run_test checking the remaining objects in each osp and rollover that osp if below some threshold, e.g. LOV_MAX_STRIPE_COUNT. let's open a new jira ticket for this.

Patch Set 1:

Failed optional test review-dne-part-9 on RHEL 9.3/x86_64 uploaded by Onyx Autotest 2 from onyx-81vm4: https://testing.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/c3725364-75f0-4350-8697-8887cf15adb2 ran 9 tests.  2 tests failed: racer, parallel-scale.

Patch Set 1:

Passed enforced test full-part-2 on RHEL 8.9/x86_64 uploaded by Onyx Autotest 2 from onyx-71vm4: https://testing.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/88611a36-2fb5-4544-a2ab-57df8d123715 ran 12 tests.

Patch Set 1:

> I also thought about getting rid of force_new_seq_all for the
 > overstriping tests, one way would be in run_test checking the
 > remaining objects in each osp and rollover that osp if below some
 > threshold, e.g. LOV_MAX_STRIPE_COUNT. let's open a new jira ticket
 > for this.

I think that is just working around the issue in the test harness for what is a problem in the code.  Yes, it will fix the test failures, but we will just have to fix it at some point in the future for the user only getting 40 stripes on their file instead of 400 on their overstriped file because the OST SEQ was wrapping.

At least if we call "force_new_seq()" in the failing subtests it is "more obvious" that it is doing something, and it won't add overhead to each of the 15000 other subtests that run and *don't* care about this case (and possibly change test behavior, maybe hiding a SEQ rollover bug because it will never happen "in the wild").

commit.subject: LU-11912 tests: add force_new_seq for sanity/130g
commit.message:
LU-11912 tests: add force_new_seq for sanity/130g

We use force_new_seq for the test cases using overstripe
to make sure we have enough objects in the current
precreate pool, e.g. sanity/27Cd.
sanity/130g does the same but the number of objects
required depends on the number of OSTs.

Change-Id: I129b3e6c3f7847cfd20be270f558717c98e9f439
Test-Parameters: testgroup=full-part-2
Fixes: 2fdb1f8d01 ("LU-11912 tests: SEQ rollover fixes")
Signed-off-by: Li Dongyang <dongyangli@ddn.com>
